ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Kale, cooked) used as closest parent; no specific entry for Thai-style stir-fried kale dish exists in FoodEx2.

allergens: gluten (from soy sauce and vegetarian oyster sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Thai-style vegetarian stir-fried kale is approximately 250 g, typically served as a side dish or main vegetable accompaniment with steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried Chinese kale (Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra) with garlic and oyster sauce — adapted from USDA FoodData Central: Kale, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC ID 169238) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for use in East Asia, with adjustment for stir-frying oil and sauce additions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai-style vegetarian stir-fried kale exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central cooked kale baseline (FDC ID 169238) with standard stir-fry oil addition (~5 g vegetable oil per 100 g dish) and soy/oyster sauce sodium contribution (~300 mg sodium per 100 g dish). Protein contribution from tofu or mushroom optional; baseline reflects kale-dominant composition. FAO/INFOODS SEA used to cross-check micronutrient plausibility.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of non-starchy vegetables and mixed vegetable stir-fry dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked kale and non-starchy vegetable GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai-style vegetarian stir-fried kale. GI estimated at 32 based on cooked non-starchy leafy vegetables (kale, Chinese broccoli) from the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently report GI values in the 25–40 range for cooked leafy greens. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 18.75) / 100 ≈ 6.0, using 250 g serving with 7.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and vegetarian oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that this dish contains approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1050 mg per standard 250 g serving) due to soy sauce and vegetarian oyster sauce; requesting reduced-sauce preparation or limiting portion size is advisable to keep daily sodium within the recommended 2000 mg limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre from kale
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 32 and a glycemic load of approximately 6 per serving, making it a suitable vegetable dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the high dietary fibre content from kale further supports postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high vitamin K and antioxidants from kale, moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, with kale providing vitamin K, vitamin C, and antioxidants beneficial for cardiovascular health; however, the moderate sodium content from sauces warrants attention for individuals managing cardiovascular disease, and reduced-sodium soy sauce variants are recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Stir-fried Thai-style Kale (ผัดคะน้าเจ, Phat Khana Je) is a classic Thai vegetable dish made by wok-frying Chinese kale (kai-lan, Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra) with garlic, vegetarian oyster sauce, and light soy sauce over high heat. The dish is a vegetarian adaptation of the widely popular Pad Kana (ผัดคะน้า), traditionally made with pork or seafood, substituting animal proteins with tofu, mushrooms, or simply the kale alone. The cooking technique uses high-heat wok stir-frying, which preserves the bright green colour and crisp-tender texture of the kale while imparting a characteristic wok hei (breath of the wok) aroma. The flavour profile is savoury and umami-rich from the sauces, with a mild bitterness from the kale and a fragrant garlic base. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at Thai restaurants as a side vegetable dish accompanying steamed jasmine rice. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-GI dish providing approximately 85 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful dietary fibre (2.4 g/100 g), vitamin C (28 mg/100 g), vitamin A (60 mcg RAE/100 g), calcium (95 mg/100 g), and iron (1.2 mg/100 g) from the kale. The primary nutritional concern is sodium content (approximately 420 mg/100 g) from soy and oyster sauces, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is suitable for vegetarians, dairy-free diets, and individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic index and high fibre content. It is not suitable for those with soy or gluten allergies without modification.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai-style vegetarian stir-fried kale exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central cooked kale baseline with standard stir-fry oil and sauce additions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for cooked non-starchy leafy vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is transparent, the ingredient composition is well-understood, and the analogous sources are approved primary sources.
notes: 1. The food name in the input record ('609D Vegetarian Stir-fried Thai-style Ka') appears to be a truncated menu item name; the canonical English name has been expanded to 'Vegetarian Stir-fried Thai-style Kale' based on the dish identity (kale / kai-lan / kana is the primary vegetable). The '609D' prefix is a menu item code from Sanook Kitchen - Funan and is not part of the canonical food name. 2. Nutrient values are estimated; no exact match found in primary regional sources. USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169238 (Kale, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt) used as the kale baseline, with additions for stir-fry oil (~5 g/100 g dish) and soy/oyster sauce sodium (~300 mg/100 g dish). 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients in the baseline formulation; confirmed zero for plant foods per USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as kale and plant-based sauces contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked kale. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a vegetarian dish with no animal-derived fat; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as vegetable oils used in stir-frying do not contain significant trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for vegetable oils. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value estimated at 2.1 g based on vegetable oil fatty acid profile), polyunsaturated_fat_g estimated at 1.8 g. 5. GI set to 32 as an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for cooked non-starchy leafy vegetables; gi_category is low. 6. This dish is vegetarian but not confirmed vegan (vegetarian oyster sauce may contain trace egg white in some formulations) and not confirmed halal (soy sauce and oyster sauce brands vary). 7. The dish is served at Sanook Kitchen - Funan, a Thai restaurant in Singapore. Price SGD 10.68 per serving.
